question: Explain the difference between a Docker image and a container.
shortAnswer: An image is a read-only template (filesystem layers + metadata). A container is a running instance of an image — it adds a writable layer on top. You can run multiple containers from the same image, each with its own state. Analogy: image is the class, container is the instance.
detailedAnswer:
Image:
- Read-only filesystem layers (each Dockerfile instruction = layer)
- Immutable — building always produces the same result
- Stored in registries (Docker Hub, private registry)
- Shared across containers (layer deduplication)

Container:
- Running process with its own writable layer on top of the image
- Has its own network, PID namespace, filesystem view
- Ephemeral — when removed, writable layer is lost
- Multiple containers from same image run independently

In SGN3 context:
- WildFly image: base OS + JDK + WildFly + SGN3 WAR deployed
- Each pod in OKD runs a container from that image
- Image built once in CI → deployed to multiple environments
- Container state (logs, temp files) is ephemeral — persistent data in external DB
keyPoints: Image = read-only template (layers), Container = running instance
Containers are ephemeral — writable layer lost on removal
redFlags: Not knowing that containers are ephemeral

followUps.question: What happens to data written inside a container when it stops?
followUps.hint: Lost (writable layer removed). Use volumes for persistent data.

followUps.question: How do layers affect build performance?
followUps.hint: Unchanged layers are cached. Order Dockerfile instructions from least to most changing.

realExperience.title: SGN3 containerized deployment
realExperience.description: SGN3 runs as containerized WildFly in OKD (OpenShift). Image built in CI pipeline, deployed across HML/Prod. Multiple pods (containers) from same image for horizontal scaling.

question: How do you write an efficient Dockerfile? What are common mistakes?
shortAnswer: Key principles: multi-stage builds (build in one stage, copy artifacts to slim runtime), order instructions from least to most changing (dependencies before code), minimize layers, use .dockerignore, don't run as root. Common mistakes: copying entire project before dependency resolution (cache invalidation), using :latest tag, running as root.
detailedAnswer:
Efficient Dockerfile:

1. **Multi-stage build** — separate build from runtime:
```dockerfile
# Build stage
FROM maven:3.9 AS build
COPY pom.xml .
RUN mvn dependency:resolve  # Cached unless pom changes
COPY src ./src
RUN mvn package -DskipTests

# Runtime stage (smaller image)
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre
COPY --from=build target/app.jar /app.jar
USER 1001
ENTRYPOINT ["java", "-jar", "/app.jar"]
```

2. **Layer ordering** — least changing first:
   - OS packages → dependencies → compiled code → config
   - Changing code doesn't invalidate dependency cache

3. **Security:**
   - Non-root user (USER 1001)
   - Minimal base image (distroless or slim)
   - No secrets in image (use env vars or mounted secrets)

Common mistakes:
- `COPY . .` before `mvn dependency:resolve` (entire cache invalidated on any code change)
- Using :latest (non-reproducible builds)
- Running as root (security risk)
- Installing dev tools in production image
keyPoints: Multi-stage: build in one stage, run in slim stage
Copy dependencies before code (cache layer reuse)
redFlags: Single-stage Dockerfile with build tools in production image
COPY . . as first instruction (cache always invalidated)

followUps.question: What is a multi-stage build and why use it?
followUps.hint: Build tools (Maven, npm) only in build stage. Production image is minimal (only runtime).

followUps.question: How do you handle secrets that the build needs?
followUps.hint: Docker BuildKit --mount=type=secret. Never COPY secrets into a layer.

realExperience.title: SGN3 Docker build pipeline
realExperience.description: Multi-stage Dockerfile: Maven build stage → WildFly runtime stage with deployed WAR. Layer caching for dependencies (pom.xml changes infrequently). Non-root execution in OKD.

question: How do you use Docker Compose for local development?
shortAnswer: Docker Compose defines the entire local environment: PostgreSQL, ActiveMQ Artemis, WildFly (or app), all with consistent ports and configurations. One command (docker compose up) gives every developer the same environment. I use it to run SGN3 dependencies locally without installing each service manually.
detailedAnswer:
Docker Compose for local dev:

```yaml
services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:10
    ports: ["5434:5432"]
    volumes: [pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data]
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: senai
      POSTGRES_USER: ...

  artemis:
    image: apache/activemq-artemis
    ports: ["8161:8161", "61616:61616"]

  wildfly:
    build: .
    ports: ["8080:8080", "9990:9990"]
    depends_on: [postgres, artemis]
```

Benefits:
- Reproducible: every developer gets the same environment
- Isolated: doesn't conflict with other projects
- Disposable: `docker compose down -v` for clean state
- Fast onboarding: new developer runs one command

In SGN3: PostgreSQL + Artemis + Glowroot run via Compose. WildFly runs locally in IDE (hot reload) connecting to Compose services.
keyPoints: One command for entire local environment
Dependencies (DB, queue) in containers, app in IDE for hot reload
Volumes for persistent data, disposable for clean state
redFlags: "I install PostgreSQL directly on my machine"
Cannot explain volumes (data lost on container removal)
No local development strategy at all

followUps.question: What is the difference between docker compose up and docker compose run?
followUps.hint: up starts all services. run starts one service for a one-off command.

followUps.question: How do you handle database migrations in Docker Compose?
followUps.hint: Init scripts in volumes, or Flyway/Liquibase on startup.

realExperience.title: SGN3 local dev with Docker Compose
realExperience.description: PostgreSQL 10 + ActiveMQ Artemis in Docker Compose. WildFly runs in IDE (IntelliJ) for hot deploy. Glowroot embedded for local performance analysis. One docker compose up and the dev environment is ready.
